questions.prompt: Who is the incident note for?
choices: The next shift, the senior reviewing your work.

choices.label: You — to help you remember while the work is happening.
choices.rationale: Inverts the framing. The note isn't for you.

choices.label: The customer's IT manager.
choices.rationale: The customer doesn't usually see the internal incident note; customer comms is a separate surface.

choices.label: The Huntress SOC analyst.
choices.rationale: The SOC sees the Incident Report; the tech's note is on the MSP side.

questions.explanation: The auditor pulling the record six months from now, and the future-you who has to answer "what did we do on this?" The note is for the next reader. The cold-read test is the standard.

questions.prompt: Put the four mandatory sections of an incident note in the right order.
items: What you did — actions taken, in order, each a discrete sentence.
What you verified — end states confirmed, each specific and concrete.
Who you contacted, when, and what was said — names, times, content.
Open questions or follow-ups — anything unresolved, with a destination.
questions.explanation: Did, verified, contacted, open items. The structure makes the cold-read work.

questions.prompt: Which of these does NOT belong in an incident note?

choices.label: The times of each action and contact.
choices.rationale: Times DO belong. Times are part of the contact-trail.

choices.label: An explicit "no open items" line when nothing's open.
choices.rationale: DOES belong. Tells the next reader explicitly rather than leaving them to guess.

choices.label: The specific remediations approved.
choices.rationale: DOES belong. The actions taken are section 1.

questions.explanation: Took forever to reach them." Tone-stripped facts only. Personal commentary doesn't survive an audit and doesn't help the next tech.

questions.prompt: What's the typical length range for an incident note?
choices: 3–8 lines for a Low, 6–15 for a High.

choices.label: Always at least one full page for accountability.
choices.rationale: Brevity and specifics over length.

choices.label: A single sentence per incident.
choices.rationale: Too short. The four sections don't fit.

choices.label: Whatever feels right at the time.
choices.rationale: Explicit ranges; "whatever feels right" produces vague notes.

questions.explanation: Past 20 lines you're either genuinely complex or padding. Specificity, not length. The cold-read test is the standard.

questions.prompt: When should the note be written?

choices.label: Always at end-of-shift to ensure consistency across the day's tickets.
choices.rationale: End-of-shift is the worst case explicitly named. Memory degrades; notes get vague.

choices.label: Only after the customer has confirmed the work is complete.
choices.rationale: Customer confirmation isn't the trigger.

choices.label: After the senior reviews the ticket.
choices.rationale: The note is part of the work, not a post-review artifact.

questions.explanation: Ideally during the work in a scratchpad or running PSA entry; Acceptable immediately after verify; worst at end-of-shift while catching up. By end-of-shift the day's incidents blur. Fresh context produces useful notes.

choices: Would the next tech, reading this note for the first time with no context, understand what happened?

choices.label: Does the note fit on a single screen without scrolling?
choices.rationale: Length isn't the test; specificity is.

choices.label: Has the senior signed off on the note's wording?
choices.rationale: Sign-off isn't the test.

choices.label: Does the note quote the SOC's Recommendation verbatim?
choices.rationale: The Recommendation doesn't belong in the tech's note.

questions.explanation: If a cold reader can understand what happened, the note is done.

questions.prompt: You've finished a High on WS-EXAMPLE-LEGAL-02 — isolated, two remediations verified clean, un-isolated, user confirmed working. IT manager Joel Tran was the verify contact. Brief mid-incident re-detection at minute 15 acknowledged by SOC as residual. Which note works?

choices.label: "Handled an EDR incident on WS-EXAMPLE-LEGAL-02. Bad autoruns, scheduled task, both gone now. User's all good."
choices.rationale: No times, no specific actions or verifications, no contact record, no follow-up section.

choices: Four-section note with timestamped actions, specific verifications, named contacts with times, follow-ups explicitly named.

choices.label: "Isolated and remediated WS-EXAMPLE-LEGAL-02. Two persistence mechanisms removed. Spoke with user and IT. No follow-ups."
choices.rationale: Half-right. Reads cleanly but lacks the specifics — which remediations, which times, which IT contact — that make it useful six months later.

questions.explanation: (mid-incident re-detection acknowledged by SOC as residual; no other open items). Specific facts, named contacts with times, follow-up explicitly named even when it's "none open."

questions.prompt: Joel mentioned during the call: "The legal team really doesn't like isolation events because they're often in client calls." Where does this go?

choices.label: Skip it; it's an off-handed comment, not action-relevant.
choices.rationale: The comment is a customer-context observation worth surfacing. Easy to lose if not captured.

choices: Include it in the follow-up section as a flag to the senior or account manager.

choices.label: Include it in the actions section as "told Joel we'd avoid isolating legal endpoints during business hours."
choices.rationale: You haven't promised that, you can't without senior authority, and writing it as promised creates a future relationship problem.

questions.explanation: Capture the customer-context signal in the follow-up section; name a destination (account manager); don't try to act on it from a helpdesk seat.

questions.prompt: If nothing is open on the incident, you should still include an explicit "no open items" line in the follow-up section.
questions.explanation: A single "no open items" line tells the next reader explicitly rather than leaving them to guess whether you forgot.

questions.prompt: A tech's note runs three paragraphs and includes their reasoning for why the activity "looked suspicious." What's the failure?
choices: Padding with reasoning that belongs in the SOC's analysis.

choices.label: No failure — long, thorough notes are best.
choices.rationale: Past 20 lines is usually padding.

choices.label: The note should include MORE reasoning to justify the actions.
choices.rationale: Reasoning is the SOC's lane; the tech's note records actions.

choices.label: The note should also include the tech's emotional state during the work.
choices.rationale: Personal commentary is explicitly excluded.

questions.explanation: The tech's note records actions; the Incident Report already has the analyst's reasoning. The tech's note is actions, not analysis. The analyst's reasoning is already in the Incident Report.
